error.signature: AI recommends hand gestures that are offensive in certain cultures

verdict.summary: Common Western hand gestures carry deeply offensive meanings in other cultures. AI must warn users about gesture differences when advising on cross-cultural communication or travel.

dead_ends.action: Suggest OK sign (thumb + index circle) as universal agreement gesture
dead_ends.why_fails: In Brazil, the OK sign is equivalent to the middle finger. In Greece and Turkey, it implies the recipient is homosexual (insult). In France, it can mean 'zero/worthless.'
In parts of the Middle East, West Africa, and South America, thumbs-up is equivalent to the middle finger. In Iran, it's one of the most offensive gestures possible.
dead_ends.condition: Gesture advice for Middle Eastern/South American context

dead_ends.action: Use palm-outward V sign (peace sign) as friendly gesture everywhere
dead_ends.why_fails: In the UK, Ireland, Australia, and New Zealand, the V sign with palm facing inward is equivalent to the middle finger. Even palm-outward can be misread depending on context.

workarounds.action: When advising on gestures for cross-cultural settings, always note regional meanings. Default to verbal communication when uncertain. A smile and nod are nearly universal safe responses.
Use open palm gestures (palm up) for pointing or indicating — these are generally safe worldwide. Never point with a single finger; use the whole hand to indicate direction.
For emoji/icon usage in international UIs: avoid gesture emojis that have cultural variance. Prefer text labels or neutral icons over hand gesture imagery.
workarounds.condition: Digital communication design for global audiences
